text: Precise control over the spatial distribution of chemical functionality in two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures remains a major unsolved challenge in materials science. Although living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has been demonstrated for the fabrication of uniform 1D and 2D micelles, the epitaxial growth process of CDSA generally affords compositionally homogeneous core regions, which precludes the realization of complex multi-responsive functional materials. Herein, we develop a highly versatile seeded growth approach by employing homopolymer/block copolymer blends of different crystallizable polylactones to fabricate 2D segmented platelet micelles with distinct, concentric core domains. Through the compatible crystal lattices of different polylactones, we demonstrate the complex multiblock structures of diblock, triblock and even hexablock co-micelles and achieve desired dimensions and compositions. The structures and spatial distributions of the segmented domains are clearly identified by both atomic force microscopy (AFM) height profiling and stimulated emission depletion (STED) microscopy. Besides, due to the differences in hydrophobicity and chemistry of polylactones, the complexes can realize programmed selective hydrolytic degradation of different core segments. Therefore, the 2D NP with controlled internal chemistry and degradation behaviors has shown great potential in the development of functional materials for compartmentalized cargo delivery and controlled release applications.
